It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT moved the shape from cols 12-26 to cols 6-20, which is -6 cols (leftward). So at rows 39-50, LEFT moves the shape left by 6 cols. This is different from what I observed earlier at rows 42-53 where LEFT moved right by 6 cols.
Wait, earlier at rows 42-53, LEFT moved the shape from cols 15-29 to cols 21-35 (+6 cols, rightward). But now at rows 39-50, LEFT moved from cols 12-26 to cols 6-20 (-6 cols, leftward).
So the direction of LEFT depends on the vertical position:
- At rows 39-50: LEFT = -6 (leftward)
- At rows 42-53: LEFT = +6 (rightward)
- At rows 18-29: LEFT = +3 (rightward)
This is very confusing. Let me reconsider.
Actually, wait. The shape is now at cols 6-20, which is closer to the yellow shape at cols 3-17. If I move LEFT again, it should move to cols 0-14, which would be even closer.
But the yellow shape is at cols 3-17. To match it, the dark gray shape needs to be at cols 3-17. The shape is currently at cols 6-20. If I move LEFT by 3 cols, it would be at cols 3-17.
But at rows 39-50, LEFT moves by 6 cols. So one LEFT would move from cols 6-20 to cols 0-14, which overshoots.
Hmm, but maybe the step size changes based on the position. Let me try LEFT again and see.
Actually, I'm running very low on actions (48 of 50). Let me try to complete the level quickly.
The dark gray shape is at rows 39-50, cols 6-20. The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17.
To match the yellow shape, I need:
- Rows: 39-50 -> 42-53 (down 3 rows = 1 DOWN)
- Cols: 6-20 -> 3-17 (left 3 cols)
If I move DOWN first, the shape will be at rows 42-53, cols 6-20. Then I need to move left by 3 cols. At rows 42-53, LEFT moves right by 6 cols, and RIGHT moves left by 3 cols. So I need RIGHT to move left.
Let me try: DOWN, then RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at rows 39-50, cols 6-20
# Yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17
# Need to move down 3 rows and left 3 cols
# At rows 42-53, RIGHT moves left by 3 cols
action(['DOWN', 'RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Yellow: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

Level completed: False
